skill.name: enact-hello-js
skill.description: Runs a minimal Node.js greeting command for Enact examples and basic tool testing.
skill.summary: A small Enact example that prints a greeting and runtime details with Node.js.
skill.author: EnactProtocol

security_audit.summary: The static Ruby backtick and weak cryptography findings are false positives caused by JavaScript template literals, Markdown examples, and plain description text. A real medium-risk concern remains because SKILL.md interpolates the user-controlled name parameter directly into the command string.

medium_findings.title: User Input Interpolated Into Command Template
medium_findings.description: SKILL.md defines a command string that passes the name parameter directly into a Node.js command. If the runtime executes this through a shell without safe argument handling, crafted input could alter the command executed inside the container.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command template clearly includes user-controlled input. Confidence is medium because the exact Enact command interpolation and shell execution behavior is not shown in the files reviewed.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer Backtick Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported backtick locations are JavaScript template literals, an inline Markdown parameter name, and a Markdown bash example. No Ruby shell execution appears at these locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced code and documentation lines are visible and do not invoke shell backtick execution. They are normal JavaScript and Markdown syntax.

low_findings.title: Static Weak Cryptography Finding Is A False Positive
low_findings.description: The reported line contains the skill description text only. No hashing, encryption, checksum, or cryptographic algorithm is present at that location.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 4 is a plain YAML description string. There is no cryptographic API or weak algorithm usage in the reviewed content.

dangerous_patterns.title: Parameterized External Command
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill command launches Node.js and includes a parameter placeholder in the command string. This should be reviewed for safe argument escaping in the Enact runtime.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The external command and parameter placeholder are explicit. The files do not prove whether the runtime escapes the parameter before execution.

content.value_statement: Teams need a simple way to verify Enact tool execution with Node.js. This skill provides a small greeting command that shows input handling, timestamps, and runtime version output.
actual_capabilities: Runs a Node.js script through an Enact command definition.
Accepts an optional name parameter for a personalized greeting.
Prints a default greeting when no name is provided.
Displays an ISO timestamp for the generated response.
Reports the Node.js runtime version used by the command.
limitations: It only demonstrates a greeting workflow and does not perform broader automation.
It depends on a Node.js runtime supplied by the container image.
It does not validate or sanitize the name parameter in the script.
The command template should be reviewed before use with untrusted input.

use_cases.title: Test Enact Installation
use_cases.description: Run a minimal JavaScript skill to confirm that Enact can execute a containerized tool.

use_cases.title: Teach Skill Basics
use_cases.description: Use a compact example to show how a skill accepts input and returns terminal output.

use_cases.title: Validate Runtime Metadata
use_cases.description: Check the timestamp and Node.js version printed by a simple tool execution.

prompt_templates.title: Run Default Greeting
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the enact-hello-js skill with no name and summarize the greeting, timestamp, and runtime version.

prompt_templates.title: Run Named Greeting
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the enact-hello-js skill with the name Alice and report the greeting that it returns.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Runtime Details
prompt_templates.prompt: Run the skill twice with different names and compare the generated timestamps and Node.js version output.

prompt_templates.title: Review Command Safety
prompt_templates.prompt: Inspect how enact-hello-js passes the name parameter into its command and identify any input handling concerns.

output_examples.output: The skill returns a greeting for World, then includes the generation time and Node.js version.
The skill returns a personalized greeting for Alice with the same runtime metadata format.

output: The skill uses a parameterized command definition.
The JavaScript file prints the provided name without validation.
Review command argument escaping before accepting untrusted input.

best_practices: Use short, simple names when testing the greeting parameter.
Review command interpolation before exposing this skill to untrusted users.
Use this skill as a learning example rather than a production workflow.
anti_patterns: Do not treat this example as a complete input validation pattern.
Do not pass secrets or sensitive data as the name parameter.
Do not use it as proof that broader Enact workflows are configured correctly.

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It runs a small Node.js script that prints a greeting, a timestamp, and the Node.js version.

faq.question: Does it require a name?
faq.answer: No. If no name is provided, the script uses World as the default name.

faq.question: Is this a production automation skill?
faq.answer: No. It is best suited for examples, tests, and basic Enact demonstrations.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The command definition includes a user parameter in a command string, so safe argument handling should be confirmed.

faq.question: Does the reviewed code access the network?
faq.answer: No evidence found. The reviewed files do not contain network requests.

faq.question: Does it work with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
